reason: This text (Genesis 22) describes God's test of Abraham, commanding him to offer his son Isaac as a sacrifice. While this involves a threat of destruction, it is specifically and exclusively directed at one individual—Isaac—not a public disaster affecting the general population. The statement explicitly requires that the destruction be 'not an individual destruction, but a disaster that will affect the general public,' which directly contradicts the nature of this narrative. Furthermore, the threatened destruction is ultimately prevented by divine intervention through an angel. The text concludes with God's blessing of Abraham and his descendants, not with the execution or description of a public catastrophe.
